answer:
**Old English Elegies** (particularly *Maxims I*, ch. 6) contains direct counsel on writing as a virtuous act alongside speaking and singing: "One should speak counsel, *write runes*, sing a song, earn praise, declare glory, hasten through the day."[^1] This gnomic wisdom reflects on writing runes—likely futhorc inscriptions—as a deliberate, praiseworthy craft akin to other forms of expression.[^2] Similarly, ch. 15 (*Vainglory*) urges the listener to "inscribe in his mind the word-hoard's craft," framing inscription metaphorically as internalizing poetic wisdom.[^3]

**Cynewulf's signed poems**—*Elene*, *Juliana*, and *Andreas*—offer meta-reflections on authorship as an act of "weaving word-craft" (OE *seegan fore 'word-craeft' gewefan*) through the frail body, often concluding with the poet's runic signature of his name (Cƿēnƿulf). In *Elene* (ch. 1), Cynewulf describes composing amid sorrow: "Thus I... through this frail house have woven word-craft and wondrously gathered... I did not know the truth of the Rood until a roomier thought... unveiled wisdom in my mind’s chamber."[^4] *Juliana* (ch. 1) ends with a plea for prayers upon reciting ("who recites this poem"), tying the act to eternal judgment.[^5] *Andreas* (ch. 1) embeds similar authorial anxiety, though less explicit.[^6] These are self-aware inscriptions, where the poet reflects on writing as a redemptive struggle against mortality, embedding his name in runes (visible in transliterations).[^7]

Legal texts like *Bṛhaspati Smṛti* (ch. 1) mention scribes and signatures routinely ("signature of the debtor, witnesses, and scribe"), but without philosophical reflection on the act itself.[^8] No other works in the corpus (e.g., *Aṅgirasa Smṛti*, French political treatises) yield comparable contemplation.

[^1]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 6 (*Maxims I*), [paragraph](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/06-maxims-i/translation/#p-84cf09).

[^2]: The wiki card notes "unlocked his word-hoard" as a distinctive phrase, linking to poetic craft.[^9]

[^3]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 15, [paragraph](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/15-wilt-thu-fus-haele-fremdne-monnan/translation/#p-583181).

[^4]: *Elene*, ch. 1, [paragraph](/works/cynewulf-elene-d2d132/01/translation/#p-d2e607).

[^5]: *Juliana*, ch. 1, [paragraph](/works/cynewulf-juliana-9a2157/01/translation/#p-e769dc).

[^6]: *Andreas*, ch. 1; wiki highlights "unlocked his word-hoard" as Cynewulfian.[^10]

[^7]: Searches confirm "rune" clusters in transliterations (e.g., *Elene* ch. 1 [p-624562]); these are the poet's acrostic signatures, reflecting on inscription.

[^8]: *Bṛhaspati Smṛti*, ch. 1, [paragraph](/works/unknown-brhaspati-smrti-0fd070/01/translation/#p-dc2d5a).

[^9]: read_wiki(*unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e*), work card.

[^10]: read_wiki(*cynewulf-andreas-07b573*), work card.
